generated_note: Do not edit format_version or id/locator fields when returning to the dev AI. Only edit adult/kid/title/body/etc. text fields as instructed.

entries.adult: While you sleep, your brain files away everything from the day. Miss too many nights and it starts to show — names, meetings, where you put things. Regular sleep does more than one long lie-in ever could.

Light, meals, and bedtime are all signals your body reads to know what time it is. Scramble them and you'll feel it — in your mood, your focus, your energy. A boring wind-down routine is actually doing a lot.
Late meals shift your body clock

Your body's glucose rhythm runs on a clock. Eating a big meal late shifts that rhythm by hours — even when the calories are identical to an earlier meal. It's a timing issue, not a food issue.nnEarlier eating keeps your internal clocks in sync. Later eating pulls them apart.

Ten minutes of walking, cycling, or anything that gets you moving tends to clear the mental fog pretty reliably. It's one of the more consistent findings in cognitive research.nnYou don't need a full session. You just need to move.
Piggyback on what you already do
"After I make coffee, I do two minutes of stretching." The existing habit does the reminding — you don't have to remember anything new.nnSmall links are more durable than big resolutions.
u201cIf this, then thatu201d really works
"If it's Tuesday at 7pm, I go to the gym" outperforms "I should exercise more" every time. Specificity removes the daily decision.nnWriting the when and where is most of the work done.
Why checking a box feels good

Your brain pays attention to better-than-expected outcomes. A small visible win — a tick, a streak — registers as one. That's not shallow; it's just how learning works.nnAim that wiring at things worth repeating.

Some brains genuinely struggle to feel how long things take without external cues. Calendars, alarms, and timers aren't crutches — they're just the right tool for that particular wiring.nnVague plans fail specific people. Specificity is the fix.
Research is pretty consistent: self-criticism after a slip makes quitting more likely, not less. A calm restart keeps more people going long-term.nnFlat tyre. Fix it. Drive on.

Writing about tangled thoughts for a few minutes consistently reduces rumination in study after study. You're not trying to write well — you're just clearing the inbox.nnMessy notes count. Full sentences not required.
Regular readers carry larger vocabularies even when their formal education looks identical on paper. Fifteen minutes a day compounds over years.nnSpeed-reading trophies optional.
Mindfulness practice produces modest but reliable gains on attention measures — not magic, just practice applied to a specific skill.nnShort and daily beats long and occasional.

Spreading practice across days beats cramming it all into one session — for skills, facts, and habits alike. Your brain consolidates better with gaps in between.nnShow up regularly. Let the gaps do half the work.
Who you think you are matters
"I'm someone who exercises" sticks longer than "I'm trying to exercise more." Identity outruns motivation.nnThe story you tell on good days is the voice you hear on hard ones.
Shoes by the door. Gym bag already packed. Water bottle already filled. Environment design consistently beats willpower when you're tired.nnReduce friction. Future-you will be grateful.
Other people help you show up
Knowing someone is loosely watching — a friend, a partner, a group chat — reliably nudges follow-through. You don't need a coach.nnSometimes one text buddy is enough.
Under pressure, the brain defaults to its most familiar routines — fast. If those routines are good ones, great. If not, stress will find that out.nnSimplify the plan before stress does it for you.
The first thing poor sleep dulls is your ability to pause before reacting. Everything gets louder, smaller decisions feel bigger.nnProtecting sleep is protecting your judgement — not a soft priority.
Short breaks from sitting improve energy and help blood-sugar regulation after long desk stretches. The bar is genuinely low.nnStand up, walk to the kitchen, come back. That counts.
Real-world studies put habit formation at weeks to months, not the famous 21 days. Early repetitions are the investment. Ease is what you get later.nnYou're not slow. You're just early.

People who treat ability as buildable come back after setbacks more readily than those who see it as fixed. A bad week is noise, not a ceiling.nnThe only thing a slip proves is that you're still in the game.
Shifting the bulk of eating earlier in the day consistently shows better metabolic patterns in trials. Same food, different clock.nnTiming is a lever. Not a moral position.
Strength training supports bones, metabolism, and your ability to carry your own shopping decades from now. Two sessions a week is a legitimate longevity habit.nnNot about aesthetics. About function.

Every task switch leaves residual mental noise that slows down whatever comes next. Protecting a block of uninterrupted time isn't a luxury — it's how actual deep work gets done.nnBatch the similar stuff. Guard the focused time.
A gap is not a verdict
Missing once doesn't erase progress. Shame spirals do. The quickest recovery is the next small win — not a Monday restart ceremony.nnComebacks count as data too.
Evening light from phones delays melatonin — the signal that tells your body it's time to wind down. Your brain can't quite tell a screen from a sunny window.nnDimming helps. Distance helps more.

Mild dehydration — the kind where you don't feel particularly thirsty — is enough to measurably dent concentration and mood. Most people walk around in it.nnFull water bottle on the desk. Boring and effective.
People consistently generate more ideas while walking than sitting still. If you're stuck, a lap around the block is not procrastination.nnMove first. Judge the ideas when you're back.
Large studies show people who cycle to work have meaningfully lower heart disease rates than those who drive the same route.nnNo lycra required.
Writing a goal down with a concrete plan consistently outperforms keeping it in your head. The act of writing forces enough specificity to make it real.nnPaper is cheap accountability.

Switch tasks before finishing a thought and part of your brain stays on the old one. That leftover hum makes the new task harder than it actually is.nnJot a quick note before you jump. Costs ten seconds.
Start so small it feels silly
"Two push-ups" beats "get fit" because it's impossible to argue with. The first step should have no resistance at all.nnWin first. Scale later.
Rest is part of the program
Adaptation happens during recovery, not during effort. Skipping rest doesn't add progress — it just delays it and increases the chance something breaks.nnSchedule it. Treat it like a set.

Children in households with predictable routines show calmer, healthier patterns across multiple measures of wellbeing. Not rigid — just consistent.nnYou're building a felt sense of safety. That's the whole point.
Just logging purchases — without any budget lecture attached — tends to reduce discretionary spending on its own. Awareness does most of the work.nnThe log is the intervention.
Self-control isn't a fixed gift — it trains like anything else. Each time you follow through, the next start gets a little easier to reach.nnYou're not faking discipline; you're collecting receipts.

Sports science shows people often feel maxed out while their body still has a large reserve. The brain calls "stop" before the tank is actually empty.nnOn a brutal habit day, slow down — but question the story that you have nothing left.
Some nervous systems need louder outside cues — calendars, lists, apps — to feel time and reward fairly. That's biology, not laziness.nnUse the scaffolding that lets you live the life you chose.

The same circuits that light up for a gold star can light up for a workout or a slot machine. Healthy systems borrow that wiring on purpose.nnDirection matters more than the fact that chemistry notices.
Focus is a bundle of skills
Planning, starting, sticking, and switching are related but separate mental skills. They differ between people and can improve with practice.nnEvery steady week is training for all of them.

When rewards arrive on an unpredictable rhythm, behaviour tends to stick harder than with a clockwork prize — that's old psychology lab wisdom.nnRandom insights here echo mild curiosity — separate from your weekly treats and pot rules, not an extra game layered on top.
Knowing someone else knows your goal nudges follow-through even if they never comment.nnPick an audience you trust; visibility is the tool.

Some people genuinely mis-feel how long tasks take without clocks and reminders. That's not a character flaw — it's a mismatch between brain and vague plans.nnConcrete times and places are the kind fix.
"If X happens, I will do Y" plans routinely beat "I will try harder" in study after study.nnThe sentence is the intervention.
Rooms, layouts, and visible cues predict doing the habit better than how motivated you felt that morning.nnDesign for Tuesday-you, not Sunday-night-you.
People often follow what they think peers do — sometimes more than their own stated values.nnUse that wisely: find a group where the normal thing is showing up.
A noticeable share of adults meet criteria for attention-deficit conditions; many never get a label but still benefit from structure.nnIf a system helps you function, that's the outcome that counts.
Having several small, dependable habits correlates with life satisfaction even when the habits themselves are humble.nnRhythm is its own kind of rest.
People restart goals more often after Mondays, month beginnings, or birthdays — "temporal landmarks" in the research.nnA Wednesday miss is not a season finale; Thursday can be episode two.

Industry data suggests only a sliver of people who download habit apps are still active two months later. Logging here already puts you on the uncommon side of the curve.nnCredit the structure you built, not luck.

Plenty of animals wake on cue, but only humans set an alarm, understand what it means, and then debate whether nine more minutes is "strategic rest."nnThe snooze button is a tiny comedy about self-control every morning.
Over a life, sleep adds up to decades — more than many mortgages last. Unlike a mortgage, good sleep pays you back in mood and focus.nnTreat the mattress like infrastructure, not luxury.
The liver keeps its own clock
Roughly one standard drink per hour is what a typical liver clears — coffee, cold showers, and pep talks don't speed the chemistry.nnPatience is the only life hack here.
Eleven days awake (please do not try)
A famous 1964 stunt: eleven days awake under medical supervision. He recovered. He also said he'd never do it again.nnYour late scroll session isn't that experiment, but it rhymes.
Gyms spike in early January, then go quiet by February. March regulars rarely have more "motivation" — they just have a plan that survived February.nnSystems beat vibes.
The body moves before the story
Classic lab work found brain activity shifting before people reported a conscious choice to move. Much of life is noticing yourself mid-habit.nnAutomatic isn't the enemy — it's the finish line.
People who make the bed often report feeling more on top of the day. Causation is fuzzy; the easy win is not.nnTwo minutes for a visible "already done" badge.
Some surveys find phones checked nearly a hundred times a day — tiny hits of novelty all waking hours.nnIf your habit app feels sticky, you're competing with world-class designers. Fair fight.
A mile of jogging burns less energy than a fancy pastry contains — annoying math, true math.nnStill worth it for heart, mood, and bones; health is not a spreadsheet duel.

"Deadline" once meant a line prisoners could not cross — serious consequences. Now it means spreadsheets.nnYour week still has a firm line: logs lock after Sunday — gentler stakes, same human love of a boundary.
A well-known study found judges' leniency dipped before meal breaks and rose after — probably fatigue and timing, not cruelty.nnYour 4pm self isn't morally worse; just running low.
The "three-week habit" story came from a surgeon's guess decades ago. Real studies show weeks to many months depending on the habit and the person.nnYou're not behind — you're on a real curve.
Average humans walk enough miles in a life to circle Earth several times — mostly in small trips.nnThe "extra" walks for health are a rounding error that still changes risk.
Big studies link cycling to work with much lower heart-disease risk than driving the same route.nnLycra optional; pedals sufficient.
Muscles don't care if the weight has opinions. Carrying a toddler upstairs still counts as load.nnParenting: accidental gym.
Roman "average lifespan ~35" mostly reflects child mortality. Adults who survived childhood often reached their sixties and seventies.nnAverages hide different stories.
Most cells replace on a multi-year rhythm — you're a slow ship of Theseus.nnToday's habits feed tomorrow's replacement crew.
Putting things off often dodges boredom, anxiety, or shame — not a calendar glitch.nnName the feeling, shrink the task, start ugly.
Long-term stress nudges appetite toward quick calories — an old survival script.nnNot weakness; wiring you can plan around.
That urge to squeeze a puppy? Researchers gave it a name: cute aggression. Harmless, common, weirdly sweet.nnTotally unrelated to your streak — just a smile break.

Classic decision research found people hate losing about twice as much as they enjoy gaining the same amount. A clear "floor" uses that honestly: protecting progress hits different emotional notes than a vague "do better."nnThe app names the stakes so your attention has somewhere real to stand.nnBelow the floor, recovery keeps rules transparent — core-only pot accrual and strict-drop resets — all explicit, with no random layer on top of your treats or pot.

In real-world trials, savers who chose accounts that locked funds until a date often built bigger balances than people left totally free — even when freedom sounds nicer.nnA named pot is a soft version of that contract with yourself.

Coffee-shop studies showed people speed up purchases as a loyalty card nears full — effort rises near the finish. A filling progress bar borrows the same nudge.nnClose targets feel magnetic; use that instead of guilt.

Economists model a pattern many people feel: distant rewards shrink in our minds while tonight's treat grows huge. Pairing a small weekly win with a slow-building pot feeds both versions of you.nnStructure ends the argument instead of moralising it.

A large review of health programmes found keeping track of behaviour — food, steps, sleep — was one of the strongest ingredients that made interventions work.nnYour taps aren't theatre; they're a proven behaviour-change tool.
Michie et al. — behaviour change techniques
Earn the fun, enjoy it harder

Researchers boosted gym visits by pairing addictive audiobooks with treadmill time only. Your weekly treat after qualifying is the same bargain: joy rides on the habit, not beside it.nnPleasure with a gate stays memorable longer.

Give someone a random mug and they suddenly price it like treasure. Streaks and pots inherit that quirk — losing ground stings because it feels like giving something up.nnThe design respects that feeling instead of pretending you're a spreadsheet.

Self-regulation research says we compare "where I am" to "where I want to be," then adjust. Colours, chips, and week rows make that comparison visible and friendly.nnYou get direction without drowning in shame.
Labels change spending in your head
"Holiday money" and "bill money" feel different even in the same account. Separate goal pots ride that habit so wins stay attached to their story.nnCompartmentalising can be a feature.

Brain studies show cells that learn from "better than expected" moments. A pop-up fact you didn't schedule is a mild version of that — curiosity without turning insights into a wager.nnSmall novelty can support focus when kept kind.
The green pill around your name shows you're currently online. When it's grey, you've lost connection — but your data keeps saving on this device until the next sync.

When consistency jumps straight from struggling to solid, it usually means several closed weeks lined up behind the number — not one fluke week.nnLet the colour change be a gentle "keep going," not pressure to be perfect next week.

Early in a month, people often lean on fresh-start energy. Research on "temporal landmarks" shows Mondays and month beginnings nudge new attempts without needing a dramatic reboot.nnIf your goals sit in the steady band, you're allowed to ride that tailwind.

Big studies on habits stress boring repetition over hero weeks. A new calendar year is just another Monday in a longer chain — useful if it helps you show up, not if it becomes all-or-nothing.nnSteady beats dramatic when the year gets noisy.

Maintenance research keeps repeating one lesson: slips are normal; re-entry predicts long-term change more than a spotless streak.nnOpening the log again after a rough week is already the behaviour that matters.
Habits added mid-week get a fair start

If you add a habit on a day other than Monday, that first partial week uses proportional targets — your threshold is scaled down based on how many days remain in the week.nnFor example, if your goal is 5 days per week and you start on Thursday, you only need to hit roughly 2 days (floor of 5 × 4/7). This keeps scoring fair without blocking your goal on a week you could never fully complete.

Core habits must all qualify for a closed week to count toward your % and weekly treats; growth habits add pot money when they qualify but never block the week. In recovery (rolling % below that goal's consistency floor), only core habits add to the pot; growth adds £0 until rolling % reaches the floor again. Each goal's floor is set in Add/Edit goal (default often 80%).

Popular books like "The Molecule of More" and "Dopamine Nation" stress one idea: much of what people call "reward" is wanting and momentum, not the cosy feeling after a good meal.nnChasing the next tick is dopamine-heavy; deep rest after is often different chemistry entirely.
Two styles of dopamine, one you

Lieberman and Long describe a "here-and-now" chemical chorus and a "future-and-possible" dopamine track that loves novelty and goals — both matter.nnBalance is the game: enough future-pull to plan, enough present chemistry to enjoy arriving.
Lieberman & Long — The Molecule of More

Dopamine-related circuits learn from surprises and progress signals — "better than expected" keeps attention. That's why visible streaks and near-complete bars tug harder than abstract goals.nnYou're not weak; you're using a loud channel wisely.
Schultz et al.; Lieberman & Long (popular synthesis)
Pleasure and pain share a balance beam

Psychiatrist Anna Lembke argues endless easy hits — scrolls, snacks, buys — can tilt the brain toward needing more just to feel normal. Brief, chosen discomfort (effort, delay) resets the scale.nnHabit apps that add friction on purpose echo that clinical lesson gently.
Oxytocin is a trust hormone, not only a cuddle hormone

Studies tie oxytocin to bonding cues: touch, warm voice, shared meals. It nudges social calm and "we" feelings.nnAccountability partners and family habits may ride social chemistry, not just willpower.
Oxytocin has more than one mood

Researchers note context matters: the same hormone that supports closeness can sharpen feelings about "us versus them" in some settings.nnKindness with boundaries stays wise — chemistry is not a morality tale.
Serotonin is a stabiliser, not a smile pill

Most brain serotonin doesn't come with simple labels. It helps regulate mood, sleep timing, and even gut signalling — a steadying hand, not a happy switch.nnMedication stories are separate from daily habits; both deserve nuance.

Exercise and outdoor light are linked to better mood and sleep in large studies — serotonin-related pathways are part of the story, alongside many other systems.nnA walk is low-tech support for a complicated brain.

After intense pleasure or orgasm, prolactin often rises — part of why excitement eases and the body pivots toward rest. It's normal biology, not failure.nnUnderstanding the cooldown helps you plan recovery, not judge it.
Prolactin shows up for caregivers too
Hormone research links prolactin to nurturing behaviours in many mammals — milk production in some contexts and caregiving tone more broadly.nnBodies reward tending others with their own chemistry.

Sapolsky's "Behave" walks one behaviour through genes, hormones, context, and culture — dopamine, serotonin, oxytocin, and more act in concert like instruments, not soloists.nnWhen you change a habit, you're conducting the whole band slowly.
Books beat doom-scrolling for the same circuits

Writers like Lembke and Lieberman describe how cheap digital hits train dopamine shortcuts. Swapping some scroll time for long-form reading rebuilds patience — the same patience habits need.nnThis app's slower rewards are a cousin of that swap.

entries.kid: While you sleep, your brain tidies up the day — putting things where they belong. Miss a lot of sleep and remembering gets trickier. Going to bed at the same time most nights works better than one big sleep-in.

Sunlight, meals, and bedtime tell your body what time it is. Mixed signals can make you feel off.

A calm wind-down routine is doing real work, even if it feels a bit boring.

A very big meal late at night can make blood sugar trickier to handle — even with the same food eaten earlier in the day.

It's a body clock thing, not a "being bad" thing.

About ten minutes of walking or moving often clears a fuzzy head.

You don't need a huge workout — wiggle, walk, dance. Whatever gets you going.
Hook new habits to old ones

"After I brush my teeth, I do two stretches." The old habit reminds you — no extra remembering needed.

Tiny links beat giant promises.

"Tuesday after school I ride my bike" beats "I should move more."

Brains love a clear plan.

Your brain loves little wins — checkmarks, stars, streaks.

Use that power for habits that actually help you.

Some brains need clocks, timers, or lists to feel how long things take. That's completely fine — tools are helpers.

"Someday" is fuzzy; "after snack" is something your brain can work with.

Being mean to yourself after a slip makes it harder to try again.

Fix it like a flat bike tyre: patch it and ride on.

A few minutes of messy writing can untangle worried thoughts.

You don't need perfect sentences — just get it out.

Reading a little most days grows your vocabulary over time.

Short, cosy reading still counts.

Calm attention practice can train focus a bit like practice trains sport skills.

Short and often beats rare and long.

A bit on many days beats one giant cram — for skills and habits alike.

Gaps between tries help your brain learn.

"I am a reader" or "I move my body" can stick better than "I am trying."

Tell a story that helps you on hard days.

Lay out clothes, fill a water bottle, put shoes by the door — easy setups beat arguing with a tired brain.

Less friction, more follow-through.

When someone kind knows your plan, you're more likely to do it.

A friend, parent, or coach counts.

When stressed, brains reach for familiar routines fast — good or not.

Keep your plan simple before stress arrives.

Poor sleep makes it harder to pause before you snap or give up.

Sleep protects your choices.

Short breaks from sitting help energy and sugar levels.

Stand, stretch, grab water — it counts.

Real life habits often need weeks or months to stick — not a magic "21 days."

Early tries aren't failure — they're the start.

People who believe skills can grow bounce back after a bad day.

A rough week is a bump, not the end.

More food earlier in the day often suits the body better than only big late meals.

Timing is a tool, not a rule about "good" or "bad."

Strength helps bones and everyday jobs like carrying bags.

Two short sessions a week is a big win.

Switching tasks leaves mental "echoes" that slow you down.

Give one job a protected block of time.
A miss is not the end

Skipping once doesn't erase progress — shame spirals do more damage.

The next small win is the comeback.

Bright screens at night can delay the chemistry that makes you sleepy.

Dim screens or move them farther away before bed.

Even mild thirst can ding your mood and attention.

Keep water handy — boring and useful.

Walking often brings more ideas than sitting still.

Stuck? Try a lap before deciding.

Big studies link biking with healthier hearts than only driving.

Pedals don't need fancy clothes.

Goals with a written plan beat goals only in your head.

Paper or phone notes both work.
Finish a thought before you jump

If you switch tasks mid-thought, part of your brain stays behind.

Jot a note in ten seconds, then switch.

"Two jumps" beats "get fit forever" for actually starting.

Make step one too easy to argue with.

Muscles and skills grow during recovery, not only during effort.

Rest is part of training.

Predictable rhythms help kids feel safe and healthy — not rigid, just steady.

You're building a calm home feeling.

Just noticing what you buy often lowers extra spending — no lecture needed.

The list is the magic.
"Stop and go" skills train like sport practice — every small keep-going counts.

Bodies often have more energy than the brain admits on hard days.

Slow down, but question "I have nothing left."

Some brains need louder outside reminders. That's wiring, not laziness.

Use what works for you.

Stars, ticks, and games use similar "win" brain wiring — healthy apps borrow that on purpose.

Steer the wins toward good habits.

Planning, starting, sticking, and switching are different skills.

Steady weeks train all of them.

Random little rewards can make habits stickier than perfectly timed ones.

This app's surprise facts are gentle curiosity — separate from weekly treats and jar rules, not an extra game on top.

When a trusted person knows your goal, you're more likely to try.

They don't have to nag.

Some people mis-guess how long tasks take without a clock. That's human, not "lazy."

Concrete times fix it kindly.

"If it rains, I dance inside" beats "I will try harder."

The sentence is the superpower.

Visible cues and room layouts predict habits better than morning excitement.

Design for Tuesday-you.

People often copy what friends seem to do.

Pick friends or groups where showing up is normal.

Many people have attention differences — structure helps everyone.

If a tool helps you live your plan, it wins.

Several small, steady habits link to happier days — even humble ones.

Rhythm can feel like rest.

Mondays, month starts, and birthdays nudge new tries.

A Wednesday miss is not the finale.

Most habit apps lose people in a couple of months. Logging here already puts you on the uncommon side.

Credit your effort.

Only humans set alarms… then negotiate nine more minutes.

The snooze button is a tiny morning comedy.

Sleep adds up to a huge slice of life — like a long holiday every year.

Good sleep pays you back in mood.

Grown-up bodies clear about one standard drink per hour — coffee and pep talks don't speed it up.

Waiting is the only real shortcut.

A famous stunt: someone stayed awake for eleven days with doctors watching. He recovered — and said never again.

Don't try this. Your sleep matters.

Gyms get busy in January, then quieter. March regulars have plans that survived February.

Brains sometimes start moving before we notice we made a choice.

People who make the bed often feel more on top of the day.

Some people check their phone nearly a hundred times a day.

Still worth it for heart, mood, and bones.

Long ago a "deadline" was a line you must not cross — serious trouble. Now it means rows in an app.

Gentler stakes, same human love of a finish line.

A famous study: judges were stricter before lunch and kinder after — tired brains, not mean hearts.

Afternoon you isn't "worse," just running low.

The "three-week habit" story was a rough guess. Real habits take weeks to months.

You're not behind.

Extra walks still change health.

Lycra optional.

Muscles don't care if the weight giggles. Carrying little ones upstairs still counts.

Accidental gym.

Ancient "average lifespan ~35" mostly counted young kids dying early. Adults often lived decades longer.

Averages hide stories.

Most cells replace on a slow rhythm — you're a ship rebuilt plank by plank.

Today's habits feed tomorrow's you.

Name the feeling, shrink the task, start messy.

Long stress nudges appetite toward quick calories — old survival wiring.

Not weakness; you can plan snacks kindly.

Wanting to squeeze a puppy? Scientists called it "cute aggression" — common and harmless.

Totally unrelated to your streak — just silly joy.

People hate losing about twice as much as they enjoy gaining the same amount.

Below the floor, recovery keeps jar rules clear — core-only money until the score recovers. Ask a grown-up for the full picture.

Savers who chose "locked until a date" accounts often saved more than those with total freedom.

Goal pots here echo that gentle lock.

People speed up when a punch-card is almost full.

Progress bars use the same "almost there" pull.

Tonight's treat feels huge; faraway rewards feel tiny.

Weekly treats plus a slow-building pot feed both versions of you.

Big reviews say logging food, steps, or sleep is one of the strongest helpers in health programmes.

Your taps are real tools.

Researchers paired audiobooks with treadmill time only — gym visits went up.

Your weekly treat after qualifying uses the same idea.

We care extra about things we feel we own — streaks and pots use that.

The app respects feelings, not only numbers.

Colours and chips make that comparison kind.

"Holiday money" and "bill money" feel different even in one account.

Separate goal pots work the same way.

Brains learn from "better than expected" moments.

Surprise facts here are mild curiosity — little brain teasers, not bets.
Green around your name means you're online! Grey means you're not connected right now.

Jumping from struggling to solid usually means several good closed weeks — not one lucky week.

Let the colour change say "keep going," not "be perfect."

Month starts give many people a fresh-start boost.

If you're in the steady band, ride that tailwind.

Habits love boring repetition more than hero weeks.

A new year is useful if it helps you show up — not if it becomes all-or-nothing.

Slips are normal. What matters most is coming back.

Opening the log after a rough week already counts.

If you add a habit mid-week, your first partial week uses a smaller target so it stays fair.

Example: starting Thursday with a 5-day goal might only ask for about 2 days that week.

Core habits are your must-do habits: when a week closes, they all need to qualify so the week counts on your score and for your treat.

Growth habits add jar money when you do them — they never block the week.

In recovery (below your floor), only core adds jar money until your score climbs back; growth waits. A grown-up can help with the numbers.

A lot of what people call "reward" is wanting and go-go energy, not only cosy afterglow.

Chasing the next tick uses different chemistry than deep rest.

"Here-and-now" chemistry and "future-and-maybe" chemistry are both real — both matter.

Balance planning with enjoying the moment.

Brains learn from surprises and progress signals.

Visible streaks use a loud, honest channel — not a weakness.

Endless quick hits — scrolls, snacks — can tilt the brain toward needing more to feel normal.

Chosen effort and breaks can reset the scale.

Oxytocin links to bonding cues: touch, warm voices, shared meals.

Family habits ride social chemistry, not only willpower.

The same hormone can nudge closeness or "us vs them" feelings, depending on context.

Kindness plus boundaries stays wise.

Serotonin helps mood, sleep timing, and even gut signals — a steadying hand, not a simple "happy switch."

Medicine stories are separate from daily habits.

A walk is simple support for a complicated brain.

Understanding the cooldown helps you plan rest, not worry.

One behaviour involves genes, hormones, context, and culture together — like a band, not one instrument.

Changing a habit slowly conducts the whole band.

This app's slower rewards echo that gentler pace.

entries.adult: Without signing in, your data lives only on this device. Sign in to access it from any browser or phone.
entries.kid: Without sign-in, everything stays on this tablet or computer. A grown-up can sign in to open the same profiles on another device.

entries.adult: Sync this device with the cloud (uploads your full app data for this account)
entries.kid: Cloud sync (grown-up): keeps this device matched with your online account — uploads everything for your signed-in account.

entries.adult: A personal habit tracker built around weekly consistency — for anyone who wants to show up for themselves and actually stick with it.
entries.kid: A friendly habit app for weekly habits. For anyone who wants to show up for themselves and keep it up.

entries.adult: Save your data locally. Import only adds the profiles you pick from the file as new profiles — nothing already on this device is overwritten.

entries.adult: This only adds copies of the profiles you choose. Everything already on this device stays exactly as it is (even if an ID in the file matches one here — that copy gets a new ID).
entries.kid: This only adds profile copies you choose. Everything already on this device stays the same (even if an ID in the file matches — that copy gets a new ID).

entries.adult: We keep up to two recent backups in your account when you sync — handy if something ever looks off. Tap a saved backup to restore it.
entries.kid: We save two backups of your stuff when you sync. If something looks wrong, tap one to go back to how it was before today.

entries.kid: Build good habits, one week at a time.
Small steps, every week — and they add up faster than you think!

entries.adult: Here's how it all fits together.

Consistency works best with a little structure. This app gives you three things to keep you on track:

  • Goal — a long-term reward to keep your eyes on the big picture.
  • Weekly treat — a small reward so you feel the win often.
  • Consistency — a streak worth protecting.

Consistency is really just doing things repeatedly — nothing magic about it. But we've made it satisfying. Life gets in the way sometimes, and that's fine. Hitting 80% of the time? That's genuinely good.
Stay consistent for 80% of your weeks and you'll unlock your big Goal reward — plus you'll have actually built the habits that got you there.

A week counts when your core habits are done. We'll explain more as you go.

For now — let's set your first Goal.

entries.kid:
This app uses three ideas:

  • A big Goal to save toward.
  • A small weekly Treat when you do well.
  • A Consistency streak you'll want to protect.

Ask a grown-up if anything feels tricky.
Consistency means showing up again and again. This app tries to make that feel fair and a bit fun. Aim to be on track most weeks — grown-ups often use about 80% as a guide.
When your week qualifies, you stay Consistent and move closer to your big Goal — while building habits along the way.

A week qualifies when its core habits qualify. Grown-ups can read more in Settings → Help.

Next: add your first Goal.

entries.adult: Pick something you're genuinely working toward — the kind of thing worth saving up for over weeks or months. Make it feel like a real destination, not a to-do.

entries.kid: Think of something you really want to work toward — maybe something you'd love to save up for. Pick something that gets you excited!

entries.adult: A small treat for a good week — like a film night or a favourite snack. It should feel like a genuine reward, not a health target. Goals and treats are for staying on track, not for outcomes like losing weight.

entries.kid: Pick something fun for a good week — like a game, a snack, or a movie night. Keep it small and enjoyable, not a big task or a goal.

entries.adult: Go for specific, repeatable actions — "walk 20 minutes" or "read before bed" work well. Avoid broad outcomes like "lose 30kg", and keep your weekly treat separate from your habits.

entries.kid: Write a small thing you do again and again — like "read 10 minutes". Big goals and treats go in the other boxes.

entries.adult:
Core — Every core habit must qualify in a week for that week to count as "on track".

Growth — Both core and growth habits add money to your pots at the end of the week when they qualify.

Recovery mode — When your consistency % drops below the floor, only core habits add to your pot until you recover (more on this later).

entries.kid: Core or growth — what's the difference?

Core habits are the big “must-do” ones for a goal. After a week closes, every core habit for that goal needs to qualify so that week counts on your score circle and for your weekly treat.

Growth habits are extra helpful habits. When they qualify, they add jar money — but missing them never blocks the week from counting.

Recovery means your rolling score dropped below your floor (grown-ups pick the number per goal; it is often around 80%). Then only core habits add jar money until your score climbs back to the floor; growth habits wait and add £0 so you focus on the must-dos first.

Ask a grown-up if you want help reading the Grace screen — it explains easy-start weeks.
